description: Figure 11 presents a schematic process-flow diagram illustrating five distinct thermomechanical processing routes (labeled 1 through 5) for manufacturing austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel strips, covering both hot-rolled (HR) and cold-rolled (CR) product variants. The left side depicts hot rolling reheated to 1100–1200°C with a finish rolling temperature (FRT) of 900°C, followed by divergent cooling paths: Route 1 (dashed line) indicates direct fast cooling to an intermediate temperature and subsequent slow cooling or isothermal holding, while Route 2 shows fast cooling to room temperature followed by isothermal annealing. The center-right side illustrates cold-rolled processing, where strip undergoes cold rolling (CR) followed either by continuous annealing at 600–900°C for non-age-hardenable grades, or by solution treatment at 900–1100°C in the single-austenite region and quenching for age-hardenable alloys. Routes 3, 4, and 5 denote aging variants after solution treatment: Route 3 proceeds directly to aging without isothermal holding, whereas Routes 4 and 5 include isothermal holds intended to maximize age-hardening effects via κ′-carbide precipitation, commonly performed at 500–700°C for 5–20 h. The diagram emphasizes that post-hot-rolling cooling rates must be sufficiently high to avoid intergranular κ-carbide formation, and that process selection depends on whether the target microstructure requires precipitation hardening or recovery/recrystallization annealing.
